text_matching.label: Text Matching
text_matching.summary: Learn how `LIKE` works, how `%` and `_` wildcards behave, and how to search for partial text inside customer data.

searching_partial_text.title: How do you search partial text?

like_city_chi.title: Match cities that start with Chi

intro_to_like.title: `LIKE` helps search text by pattern instead of full exact value.
intro_to_like.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes the full text value is not known, or only part of it matters.

You may want to find:

- names that start with one letter
- cities that begin with `Chi`
- emails that end with `example.com`

That is where `LIKE` helps.

Code example:

~~~sql
SELECT full_name, city
FROM customers
WHERE city LIKE 'Chi%';
~~~

This keeps rows where the city begins with `Chi`.

So the big idea is:

> `LIKE` is used for pattern matching in text

### Try it yourself

Look at this query:

~~~sql
SELECT full_name, email
FROM customers
WHERE email LIKE '%example.com';
~~~

Ask yourself:

- which part of the email is fixed?
- which part can vary?
- why is `LIKE` a better fit here than an exact full match?

percent_wildcard.title: `%` means any number of characters.
percent_wildcard.bodyMarkdown:
The `%` wildcard can stand for:

- zero characters
- one character
- many characters

That makes it useful for searching prefixes, suffixes, or text inside a value.

Code example:

~~~sql
SELECT full_name
FROM customers
WHERE full_name LIKE '%ia%';
~~~

This keeps names that contain `ia` anywhere inside them.

So the key idea is:

> `%` means any amount of text

### Try it yourself

Compare these patterns:

- `Chi%`
- `%example.com`
- `%ia%`

Ask yourself:

- which one searches the beginning?
- which one searches the ending?
- which one searches somewhere in the middle?

underscore_wildcard.title: `_` means exactly one character.
underscore_wildcard.bodyMarkdown:
The `_` wildcard is more specific than `%`.

It stands for exactly:

- one character

That makes it useful when the pattern length matters.

Code example:

~~~sql
SELECT full_name, city
FROM customers
WHERE city LIKE 'Miam_';
~~~

This keeps rows where the city matches `Miam` plus exactly one more character.

So the key idea is:

> `_` means one and only one character

### Try it yourself

Look at these two patterns:

- `Miam_`
- `Miam%`

Ask yourself:

- which one is stricter?
- which one allows many extra characters?
- when would the one-character wildcard be more useful than `%`?

searching_partial_text.title: Partial text search helps when only part of the value is known.
searching_partial_text.bodyMarkdown:
A partial text search is useful when only one piece of the data is known.

For example, you may know:

- the start of a city name
- the end of an email address
- a short piece inside a full name

Code example:

~~~sql
SELECT full_name
FROM customers
WHERE full_name LIKE '%li%';
~~~

This keeps names that contain `li` somewhere inside them.

So the big idea is:

> partial text search lets rows be found without the full exact value

### Try it yourself

Read this query:

~~~sql
SELECT full_name, status
FROM customers
WHERE full_name LIKE 'L%';
~~~

Ask yourself:

- which part of the name is fixed?
- which part can vary?
- what kind of names should appear in the result?

m4_s01_like_city_chi.title: Search cities with LIKE
m4_s01_like_city_chi.prompt:
The SQLite workspace already has a table named `customers`.

Write a query that shows the `full_name` and `city` columns for customers whose `city` starts with `Chi`.

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: Use `LIKE` when pattern matching is needed instead of an exact full-text match.
help.hint_1: Select `full_name` and `city`, then match city values that begin with `Chi`.
help.hint_2: Build a two-column query on `customers`, then use a text pattern that keeps city values beginning with `Chi` and allows any remaining characters after that start.

m4_s01_like_city_chi.starterCode: -- Show customers in cities that start with Chi

m4_s01_email_example_domain.title: Search email endings
m4_s01_email_example_domain.prompt:
The `customers` table already exists.

Write a query that shows the `full_name` and `email` columns for customers whose `email` ends with `example.com`.

Then run it.

help.concept: The `%` wildcard is useful when the unknown text comes before or after a known piece.
help.hint_1: Select `full_name` and `email`, then match emails that finish with `example.com`.
help.hint_2: Write a two-column query on `customers`, then use a text pattern that allows any beginning before the fixed ending `example.com`.

m4_s01_email_example_domain.starterCode: -- Show emails that end with example.com

m4_s01_city_miami_single_char.title: Use the _ wildcard
m4_s01_city_miami_single_char.prompt:
The `customers` table already exists.

Write a query that shows the `full_name` and `city` columns for customers whose `city` matches the pattern `Miam_`.

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: The `_` wildcard stands for exactly one character.
help.hint_1: Select `full_name` and `city`, then use the pattern `Miam_` with `LIKE`.
help.hint_2: Build a two-column query on `customers`, then use the one-character wildcard so the city must match `Miam` plus exactly one more character.

m4_s01_city_miami_single_char.starterCode: -- Show customers whose city matches Miam_

m4_s01_name_contains_ia.title: Search inside a name
m4_s01_name_contains_ia.prompt:
The `customers` table already exists.

Write a query that shows only the `full_name` column for customers whose `full_name` contains `ia` somewhere inside it.

Then run it.

help.concept: To search for text inside a value, put the known text in the middle of a pattern and allow text before and after it.
help.hint_1: Select only `full_name`, then search for names containing the text `ia`.
help.hint_2: Write a one-column query on `customers`, then use a pattern that allows any text before and after the letters `ia`.

m4_s01_name_contains_ia.hint: Use `%` on both sides of the text.

m4_s01_name_starts_with_l.title: Search names by first letter
m4_s01_name_starts_with_l.prompt:
The `customers` table already exists.

Write a query that shows the `full_name` and `status` columns for customers whose `full_name` starts with `L`.

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: A prefix text search uses `LIKE` with the known starting text followed by `%`.
help.hint_1: Select `full_name` and `status`, then match names that begin with `L`.
help.hint_2: Build a two-column query on `customers`, then use a pattern that fixes the first letter as `L` and allows any remaining characters after it.

m4_s01_name_starts_with_l.hint: Use a prefix pattern starting with L.
m4_s01_name_starts_with_l.starterCode: -- Show customers whose names start with L

m4_s01_text_matching_project.title: Practice text matching
m4_s01_text_matching_project.prompt:
Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice text matching on the `customers` table.

For each task:

1. type the query in the editor
2. click **Run**
3. read the **Results** tab
4. check whether the returned text matches the pattern you intended

Tasks:

1. Show `full_name` and `city` for customers whose `city` starts with `Chi`
2. Show `full_name` and `email` for customers whose `email` ends with `example.com`
3. Show `full_name` and `city` for customers whose `city` matches `Miam_`
4. Show only `full_name` for customers whose `full_name` contains `ia`
5. Show `full_name` and `status` for customers whose `full_name` starts with `L`

Run one query at a time.
This project is about using `LIKE`, `%`, and `_` to search text patterns clearly.

help.concept: This project builds the core pattern-matching habits: searching by prefix, suffix, single-character pattern, and partial text.
help.hint_1: Some tasks need text at the start, some at the end, and some in the middle. Choose the wildcard pattern that matches that meaning.
help.hint_2: Before writing each query, say the text pattern in plain English first. Then build the `LIKE` pattern so it matches that shape exactly.

m4_s01_text_matching_project.hint: Match the wildcard pattern to the text shape you want.
m4_s01_text_matching_project.starterCode:
-- Task 1
-- Show customers in cities that start with Chi

m4_s01_like_meaning.title: What does LIKE do?
m4_s01_like_meaning.prompt: What is the main job of `LIKE` in SQL?

help.concept: `LIKE` is used for pattern matching in text values.
help.hint_1: Think text patterns instead of exact full matches.
help.hint_2: It helps when part of a text value is known, not necessarily the whole exact value.

m4_s01_percent_meaning.title: What does % mean?
m4_s01_percent_meaning.prompt: In a `LIKE` pattern, what does `%` usually mean?

help.concept: `%` stands for any number of characters.
help.hint_1: Think any amount of text.
help.hint_2: It can stand for zero, one, or many characters.

m4_s01_underscore_meaning.title: What does _ mean?
m4_s01_underscore_meaning.prompt: In a `LIKE` pattern, what does `_` usually mean?

help.concept: `_` stands for exactly one character.
help.hint_1: Think one character only.
help.hint_2: It is more specific than `%` because it matches a fixed length of one character.

m4_s01_partial_search_meaning.title: What is partial text search?
m4_s01_partial_search_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of searching partial text in SQL?

help.concept: Partial text search means matching part of a value instead of requiring the full exact text.
help.hint_1: Think part of the word or phrase.
help.hint_2: It is useful when you know only the beginning, ending, or inside piece of a text value.

m4_s01_partial_search_meaning.hint: Think only part of the value is known.

options.a: Matching only part of a text value
options.c: Returning every row without checking text

m4_s01_like_result_check.title: How should you check a LIKE result?
m4_s01_like_result_check.prompt: After running a query with `LIKE`, what is a strong habit?

help.concept: You should verify that the returned text really matches the pattern you intended.
help.hint_1: Think compare the rows to the pattern.
help.hint_2: Do not just trust the query. Check whether the returned values fit the prefix, suffix, or partial-text rule.

m4_s01_like_result_check.hint: Check that the text fits the pattern.

options.a: Verify that the returned text matches the intended pattern

m4_s01_like_true.title: What is true about LIKE?
m4_s01_like_true.prompt: Select all true statements about `LIKE` in SQL.

help.concept: `LIKE` is used for text pattern matching.
help.hint_1: Think searching by shape instead of exact full value.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about pattern matching, not sorting or counting.

options.a: `LIKE` is used for text pattern matching
options.c: `LIKE` is useful when you do not know the full exact value

m4_s01_percent_true.title: What is true about % ?
m4_s01_percent_true.prompt: Select all true statements about `%` in `LIKE` patterns.

help.concept: `%` is the wildcard for any number of characters.
help.hint_1: Think flexible text length.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about prefix, suffix, and inside-text matching.

options.c: `%` is useful for prefix and suffix searches
options.e: `%` can be used on both sides of known text

m4_s01_underscore_true.title: What is true about _ ?
m4_s01_underscore_true.prompt: Select all true statements about `_` in `LIKE` patterns.

help.concept: `_` matches exactly one character.
help.hint_1: Think fixed single-character placeholder.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about one-character matching, not unlimited matching.

options.c: `_` is useful when pattern length matters
options.e: `_` can help match one missing letter position

m4_s01_partial_search_true.title: What is true about partial text search?
m4_s01_partial_search_true.prompt: Select all true statements about partial text search in SQL.

help.concept: Partial text search is about matching part of a text value rather than the whole exact value.
help.hint_1: Think starts with, ends with, or contains.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about finding text from a known piece.

m4_s01_partial_search_true.hint: Think matching a known piece of text.

options.a: You can search for text at the beginning of a value
options.b: You can search for text at the end of a value
options.c: You can search for text inside a value
options.d: Partial text search only works for numbers
options.e: Wildcards help partial text search work

m4_s01_text_matching_workflow_true.title: What matches the right workflow?
m4_s01_text_matching_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good workflow for text matching.

help.concept: A good workflow is to describe the pattern in plain English, choose the wildcard shape, run the query, and verify the text.
help.hint_1: Think prefix, suffix, middle, or exact length.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that help translate text-search meaning into the right `LIKE` pattern.

m4_s01_text_matching_workflow_true.hint: Describe the pattern first, then verify it.

options.a: Say the text pattern in plain English first
options.b: Choose `%` or `_` based on the pattern shape
options.d: Run the query and check that the returned text fits the pattern
options.e: Ignore the actual returned text after running

m4_s01_reorder_like_purpose.title: What LIKE does
m4_s01_reorder_like_purpose.prompt: Reorder the ideas to explain LIKE.

help.concept: LIKE is used for text pattern matching.
help.hint_1: Start with not needing the full value.
help.hint_2: End with matching by pattern.

m4_s01_reorder_like_purpose.hint: Think part of the text, then pattern match.

tokens.t1: sometimes you do not know the full text value
tokens.t2: you can describe the text by a pattern instead
tokens.t3: `LIKE` is used to match that pattern

m4_s01_reorder_percent_logic.title: How % works
m4_s01_reorder_percent_logic.prompt: Put the wildcard ideas in order.

help.concept: % stands for any amount of text.
help.hint_1: Think flexible length.
help.hint_2: It can match many different text shapes.

m4_s01_reorder_percent_logic.hint: Think zero, one, or many characters.

tokens.t2: `%` can also stand for one or many characters
tokens.t3: that makes it useful for flexible text matching

m4_s01_reorder_underscore_logic.title: How _ works
m4_s01_reorder_underscore_logic.prompt: Reorder the ideas to explain the underscore wildcard.

help.concept: _ stands for exactly one character.
help.hint_1: Think fixed length of one.
help.hint_2: It is more specific than %.

tokens.t3: it helps when the pattern length matters

m4_s01_reorder_partial_search_logic.title: Partial text search
m4_s01_reorder_partial_search_logic.prompt: Put the partial-search idea in order.

help.concept: Partial text search is useful when you know only part of a value.
help.hint_1: Think start, end, or inside piece.
help.hint_2: You do not need the full exact value.

m4_s01_reorder_partial_search_logic.hint: Think known piece of the text.

tokens.t1: sometimes you know only part of the text
tokens.t2: you can search by that known piece
tokens.t3: partial text search finds rows without the full exact value

m4_s01_reorder_text_matching_workflow.title: Good text-matching workflow
m4_s01_reorder_text_matching_workflow.prompt: Reorder the workflow for text matching.

help.concept: A good workflow is describe the pattern, choose the wildcard shape, run, verify.
help.hint_1: Pattern meaning comes first.
help.hint_2: Verification comes last.

tokens.t1: describe the text pattern in plain English
tokens.t2: choose `%` or `_` to match that pattern shape
tokens.t3: run the query and verify the returned text

m4_s01_fill_like_keyword.title: Pattern-matching keyword
m4_s01_fill_like_keyword.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct keyword.

help.concept: LIKE is the SQL keyword used for pattern matching.
help.hint_1: It is used instead of exact matching.
help.hint_2: The answer is LIKE.

m4_s01_fill_like_keyword.template: The SQL keyword used for text pattern matching is ____.

m4_s01_fill_percent_meaning.title: Meaning of %
m4_s01_fill_percent_meaning.prompt: Choose the best phrase to complete the sentence.

help.concept: % means any number of characters.
help.hint_1: Think flexible text length.
help.hint_2: The answer is any number.

m4_s01_fill_percent_meaning.template: In a LIKE pattern, `%` means ____ of characters.

m4_s01_fill_underscore_meaning.title: Meaning of _
m4_s01_fill_underscore_meaning.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best phrase.

help.concept: _ means exactly one character.
help.hint_1: It is more specific than %.
help.hint_2: The answer is one character.

m4_s01_fill_underscore_meaning.template: In a LIKE pattern, `_` means exactly ____.

m4_s01_fill_partial_search_need.title: When partial search helps
m4_s01_fill_partial_search_need.prompt: Choose the best word to complete the sentence.

help.concept: Partial search helps when only part of the value is known.
help.hint_1: Think not the full value.
help.hint_2: The answer is part.

m4_s01_fill_partial_search_need.hint: Think only some of the text.
m4_s01_fill_partial_search_need.template: Partial text search is useful when you know only ____ of the value.

m4_s01_fill_pattern_check.title: What to verify after LIKE
m4_s01_fill_pattern_check.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best word.

help.concept: After LIKE, you should verify that the text matches the intended pattern.
help.hint_1: Think text shape.
help.hint_2: The word is pattern.

m4_s01_fill_pattern_check.template: After running a LIKE query, verify that the returned text fits the intended ____.
